Week of 3/2 - 3/20

Weekly Market Summary

By Noah Halfon

Global Weekly Market Movements

U.S. Equities

  • S&P 500 Index: 6,506.48 (-2.52%)
    • SPX closes out the week down more than two percent as a raging Iran war and weak macro data cause investor selloffs. Most sectors ended the week red, led by a -3.90% in XLB materials and -2.71 in XLP consumer staples. XLE energy led gains rising 3.22% as higher oil prices means higher energy costs.

  • Dow Jones Industrial Average: 45,577.47 (-2.42%)
  • Russell 3000 Index: 3,696.78 (-2.44%)

U.S. Monetary & Fiscal Policy

Help for Hormuz: President Trump has once again asked for help from other nations to secure the Strait of Hormuz, as US allies so far have not offered aid to help the passage of ships. Holding 1/5th of the world's oil supply, the closing of the Strait has resulted in crude prices topping $100, with major supply disruptions being heard around the world.

//curb energy markets with Trump waiving a shipping mandate to lower the cost of transporting energy goods

//tehran warns that energy assets in the gulf are legitimate targets

Global Macroeconomic News

CPI: US CPI comes in at 2.4% vs the 2.4% expected. Core CPI also came in line with expectations at 2.5% vs the 2.5% expected. This was a much needed report as previous PPI and PCE measures missed expectations, dropping rate cut probabilities for March down to below 2%.

PPI: US PPI comes in way above expectations with the largest increase in 7 months. Headline came in at 3.4% vs the 3.0% expected, while Core PPI came in at 3.9% vs the 3.7% expectations. Last month PPI also rose 0.5%, this time with major increases led by services costs. This headline reading is also reported before any oil shocks, and you can only imagine the next reading if crude prices stays elevated. Rate cut odds for later in the year fall as inflation worries rise among rising costs.

Beyond the Barrel: As the Strait closed and crude prices rose, the primary trade was to crowd into either oil calls or big energy like Exxon or Shell. While the war continues, headline commodities aren't the only thing being affected. We have already seen the airfare industry being affected as jet fuel remains a major operating expense. Jet fuel crack spreads (price gap between jet fuel and crude oil) continue to rise, as airlines this week have also posted new guidance to calm investors.

//graph shows jet fuel refining margin in Europe/Mediterranean relative to intercontinental exchange (ICE) Brent Crude

Asia also appears to be entering an energy crisis, given that the Strait of Hormuz carries roughly a quarter of global seaborne energy trade. The International Energy Agency has already scheduled releases of 400 million barrels from emergency reserves. Specifically, countries like India are struggling as nearly 80% of households and restaurants rely on LPG for cooking. Prices for LPG have risen per cylinder, resulting in administrations like New Delhi invoking emergency powers to increase production. Russia once again capitalizes on this to sell more oil to the South Asian country, while electric cook top manufactures see shares rise. Among other effects, Amazon India reported a 15% increase in ready to eat meals, while Citi Research and Nomura have already trimmed their year end expectations for India’s NSE, Nifty 50.

This bottleneck also extends well beyond energy, disrupting commodities from fertilizer and aluminum to important semiconductor inputs like helium. Fertilizer issues could impact food production in the Gulf with 35% of raw fertilizer passing through the Strait, while a prolonged war could also mean critical demand problems for semi manufacturers. Helium is essential for semiconductor manufacturing as it helps transfer heat and cool equipment as well as maintain a clean environment during production. Most of this industrial grade helium comes from natural gas fields and is separated in processes like liquid natural gas (LNG) extraction. With the war raging on, Qatar has stopped production, shutting down its Ras Laffan facility, the world’s largest LNG plant. With Qatar holding 33% of global output and a blocked waterway, a third of the world supply is now halted. Natural gas production directly puts semiconductor supply chains at risk, where if supply shortages can outlast inventories, it would force companies to source materials at higher cost and tie up working capital. The EU alone sources 40% of helium from Qatar, while in Asia, Taiwan's TSMC handles 90% of the world's advanced chip production. As the main supplier to US Nvidia and Apple, any longer lasting impacts could hurt the US tech sector by delaying over $600B in AI investments and push energy costs up. More than 2 months of disruptions could cause helium prices to appreciate another 25-50% noted Bloomberg, with prices currently up more than four times from pre war levels.

FOMC: In a not so surprising 11-1 vote, the FOMC has kept rates unchanged. Chair Jerome Powell signals that the Fed is looking to only cut once in 2026 and possibly once in 2027. The Fed has been caught between its dual mandate, previously arguing that a strong labor market and tame inflation meant there was no need for rate cuts. New data has been quite contradictory, with January reporting a -92,000 fall in payrolls, with the previous two months revised down by -69,000 with unemployment rate rising to 4.4

//battle between inflation and labor market, but the argument was that the labor market was “stable” therefore no reason to cut. Now with inflation on the rise and jobs data showing a continuing destabilizing labor market, is stagflation going to be a problem.

//bond traders now predicting no cut until next year, as brent prices rise to 108 (3/18)

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, Washington Post, US Department of the Treasury, GuruFocus, Edward Jones
